speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is December 12, 1996. Survivor, Sidney Taussig. Interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in Huntington, New York. The language is English. Please tell us about the first picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself about from 1945, about a half a year after I came from the camp. I was, at that time, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Prague. This is after I started school again, so it was taken after-- outside of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Your sister, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Olla, who has been with me in the camp. And this picture had been taken when she was about 15. So it was taken around-- in 1947-- '46. She-- this is, again, a picture when she was going to, to school in, in Prague. She was with me in the camp, and she was working in, in the fields. And after the war, she started-- she went just for homemaking courses. There it was a homemaking school, so this is where she was going. This picture was taken at Scott Air Force Base when I was in the Air Force in Illinois. This is during the time where I was in the Radio School at Scott Air Force Base in 1951. This picture is a picture of my father taken in Florida. At that time, he was-- it was in 1984. He was 92 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything you want to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was the one, really, who saved the, the entire family because of his skills being a, a blacksmith. So I owe my life, really, to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother. It was taken in Hamilton, Ontario in 1949. My mother always was a homemaker. She was-- and working with my father in a business, keeping up all the-- all the accounting. And she-- unfortunately, she died of a heart attack, a sudden heart attack, in, in 1970. And she is-- she is missed. This is a picture of my paternal grandmother. The picture was taken sometimes in the '30s. I don't know precisely the date. She is the one who was 81 when she was brought to the camp, and who died in the camp, and whose ashes luckily we recovered and and buried in, in Prague next to my grandfather, who died in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Emily Taussig. Her maiden name was Oppenheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my paternal grandfather. His name is Edward Taussig. He was, at one time, a Grandmaster in-- a chess master. And as a young boy, I still remember going with him to places where he would play against three, four people. And he was really my, my favorite grandfather. And unfortunately, he died in-- he luckily died before, before the Germans came in 1935. And he's buried in, in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this picture-- how old you think he was when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, 80 some-- I think, 81. No, when this picture was taken-- when he died, he was 81. When this picture was taken, he must have been something in the 60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my three children. On the left is my youngest daughter, Debra. In the center is my son, Ron. And on the right is my oldest daughter, Michelle. This picture was taken in-- around 1986, 85.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken here in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in, in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Huntington, in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Huntington, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the family of my oldest daughter, Michelle, her, husband, Michael, and youngest daughter on the left, Amanda, Bryan, and Eric. The picture was taken about four or five years ago. This picture are the-- my grandchildren, my youngest daughter's children. The little-- it was taken about a week ago here in, in-- on Long Island, in Commack at a studio. The youngest one name is Taylor, the baby. The girl's name is Jacqueline. The next, on top, her oldest son, is Kyle. And brother, younger brother is Dylan. This grandson is my son's son. His name is Benjamin Edward Taussig, and he was named after my, my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document which was issued to every person who was repatriated from camps. Since nobody had any identification, the Czech government issued, through this repatriation office, passes like this for people who returned. And this a pass of my father that was issued in 1945 after we came. Everybody had a pass like that. I donated mine to the museum in Miami. And this is one from my father issued in 2/7/1945. This is the opposite side of the same document issued to my father. This is an edition of the magazine-- abbreviated edition of the magazine issued recently in Philadelphia. It was translated from Czech by a fellow from Canada. And it's-- it, it contains some of the articles from the magazine, which I described in, in my narration before. This is one of the handwritten pages, in my handwriting, from the magazine. It's not my article but because of my handwriting was illegible, really, I, I was assigned at that time to the write this article. And this is-- according to this, it's a 21st number, the second-- the second year already. That's what it says. It was issued 28/5/1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the writer was, the person who--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall who the writer was. Maybe in the original-- it is-- you know, the guy's name appears always at the end of it. I just-- I just wrote it. It is my handwriting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an article and a-- and a illustration which I had done for the magazine. It's about the crematoriums. Since I was able to go into the crematorium, I always brought the dead people to the crematorium. I had access to the crematorium. And I was able to describe the crematorium when it-- when it was built. And this is an article which I wrote.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of articles did people write? Were they-- did they write about anything under the sun? Or were-- did they try to focus on-- on the ghetto, The camp, what was going on during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the articles which appear in, in that magazine are articles which are poems. And the poems mainly reflect-- reflect current events. For example, there are poems about, about a guy who dreams about Prague when he was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are poems about a guy who's lamenting about rights. What-- are right good when they're-- what, what is the science, for example, good for when there are no rights? There is one poet-- poem in there from Hachenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: There also are limericks in it. And people usually wrote about their experiences in the camp. They also wrote some philosophical articles about Gandhi and, and whatever, whatever they felt like writing. Sometimes guys-- when they were younger, they got exposed to something, and they reflected on their-- on their youth, on their younger years when they were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the contributors were teenage boys, or were other people writing for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the contributors were guys in the home. But when the-- this magazine was put into English, there are some articles in it which, which are-- which are not exactly from, from the article. They were-- they were written on the outside or pictures. They were written outside our home. It's, it's a compilation. It's-- some-- I found some articles which don't come from that magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any adults write for the magazine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- there was one adult, who was this fellow, Taussig, who used to be on the-- on the home, too. And he came into, into our home. He wrote some of the articles also. But the majority of the articles are just from kids 13 to 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: This fellow, Taussig, is no relation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's no relation to me, no. This is a list, partial list, of, of the youngsters who went through the home. This list was compiled from memory of the guys who survived. And when we couldn't-- we couldn't really remember all the names. And it's only a partial list. And the people who survived are listed, also the people who are deceased, who couldn't-- didn't make it. I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Marion. We've been married 43 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marion, what would you like to say about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were married in 1953. I was all of 18. We were very much in love. Still are. You have to be to be-- to survive so many years. It's more like a friendship. You know, it's your best friend, your best companion. You share dreams, ups and downs, and you struggle, you survive it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he's been a devoted father, husband. What can I say? He's been my rock of Gibraltar. Any time I needed him, he's been there. We just get along very well together. What difference has it made to you that he's a survivor? How has that played out with you and your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it really did not bring [SIGHS] too much into our lives. I married him for what he was, and it just carried through. We discussed it several times. He didn't like to talk too much about it. It was a subject that was very difficult. When he was young, when we first were married, it was difficult for him to talk about it. But as the years went on, it got a little easier and easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't want to involve the children at that time. He said, why should I burden them with it? I suffered enough. But gradually, as they got older, they wanted to know a little bit. So if they asked, he responded, and he spoke with them. But he didn't press the issue or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a family. He is a very, very dedicated family man. And everything for the family. We went on vacations together. He participated in sports with the children. He was a soccer coach for my son. He encouraged the children, whatever they did. In fact, what he brought was, you have to be a survivor. Maybe that's what he brought back from the camp. He always said, you must survive. Whether it's life, whether it's education, you have to survive. If you do the best you possibly can, you're surviving. You don't always have to be at the top. You strive for it. But as long as you survive, you will get to where you are. And that was his-- that's what brought the children through all the time. And his grandchildren. He just loves his grandchildren. He loves children, period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any final remark you'd like to make about your experience during the war or anything you'd like to say to your children or grandchildren who can't be here today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my ideal in my life was my grandfather. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, I just-- No, take your time. Take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I just can't. Sorry. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: His grandfather was-- he was very close to his grandfather. He played with him. He had-- gave him time. He taught him things. And he was really very close. And he remembers him. And it just-- I guess as you get older, it just sits a little bit more with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'm really grateful to my son that he named-- [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to remember his grandfather, he-- they named him Benjamin and Edward for his grandfather, so that there is a memory there of his grandfather, which is really appreciative of-- actually, our youngest granddaughter, the little one, Taylor, is named for his mother, Rose, which is also-- we must remember that that was also another touching thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much. You're a lovely couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thank you for a wonderful interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for taking the time.
